elements.sent-2411-001: We propose a new approach for fine-grained uncertainty quantification (UQ) using a collision matrix.
elements.sent-2411-002: For a classification problem involving $K$ classes, the $K\times K$ collision matrix $S$ measures the inherent (aleatoric) difficulty in distinguishing between each pair of classes.
elements.sent-2411-003: In contrast to existing UQ methods, the collision matrix gives a much more detailed picture of the difficulty of classification.
elements.sent-2411-004: We discuss several possible downstream applications of the collision matrix, establish its fundamental mathematical properties, as well as show its relationship with existing UQ methods, including the Bayes error rate.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also address the new problem of estimating the collision matrix using one-hot labeled data.
elements.sent-2411-006: We propose a series of innovative techniques to estimate $S$.
elements.sent-2411-007: First, we learn a contrastive binary classifier which takes two inputs and determines if they belong to the same class.
elements.sent-2411-008: We then show that this contrastive classifier (which is PAC learnable) can be used to reliably estimate the Gramian matrix of $S$, defined as $G=S^TS$.
elements.sent-2411-009: Finally, we show that under very mild assumptions, $G$ can be used to uniquely recover $S$, a new result on stochastic matrices which could be of independent interest.
elements.sent-2411-010: Experimental results are also presented to validate our methods on several datasets.
elements.sent-2411-011: As machine learning is applied in high risk and high uncertainty scenarios such as healthcare \citep{healthcare and finance \citep{finance , greater interest has grown around the field of classification uncertainty quantification (UQ), which attempts to describe the uncertainty involved in a prediction task.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: UQ techniques have been developed to measure a variety of distinct phenomena using many different metrics (See Figure \ref{img:probabilitstic-vs-deterministic (B).}
elements.sent-2411-013: We propose an new uncertainty measurement, the Collision Matrix, which describes uncertainty through the probability of class collisions, defined as the same input value belonging to different classes when observed multiple times.
elements.sent-2411-014: An example of a class collision in healthcare would be two patients that arrive at a hospital complaining of identical symptoms (the input) such as muscle weakness, tingling in the extremities, and blurred vision, but where the symptoms are caused by different diseases (the class) such as Multiple Sclerosis and a Vitamin B12 deficiency.
elements.sent-2411-015: The prevalence of class collision between any two classes provides an intuitive characterization of how difficult it is to distinguish between that pair of classes, and the collision matrix contains the rate of collision collision rate between classes $i$ and $j$ in its $i,j^{\text{th }$ entry.}
elements.sent-2411-016: In Figure \ref{img:probabilitstic-vs-deterministic A, we provide an example of a $3$ class setting where two classes are easily confused and one is not.}
elements.sent-2411-017: The collision matrix identifies which pair of classes has uncertainty between them, whereas less detailed UQ measures such as the BER only indicate that there is uncertainty somewhere in the system.
elements.figure-2411-001: \label{img:probabilitstic-vs-deterministic} \begin{center} \includegraphics[width = 17cm]{fig-1-introduction_figure.pdf} \caption{ Part (A) shows the collision matrix ($S$) for a $K=3$ class classification setting where classes $1$ and $2$ are easily confused with each other, but class $3$ is not. This information is encapsulated in the collision matrix by the values in position $1,2$ and $2,1$ being larger that the other off-diagonal elements, but this information is not found in the BER. Part (B) compares the collision matrix against uncertainty quantification (UQ) measures BER \citep{BER_ensemble}, Bayesian Networks \citep{BNN_tutorial} and MC Dropout \citep{mc_dropout}.} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-019: This ability to quantify uncertainty between pairs of classes makes the collision matrix a uniquely fine-grained UQ technique and allows for new applications not available to less detailed UQ descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-020: For example, the collision matrix can be used to predict how the difficulty of a classification scenario will be affected by combining different classes together.
elements.sent-2411-021: This has the potential to make a previously impossible classification problem tractable.
elements.sent-2411-022: Returning to our healthcare example, it may not be possible to accurately predict the disease from a patients initial symptoms alone, but the collision matrix could inform the creation of broader disease categories that can be predicted with high accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-023: Another application of this fine-grained uncertainty measure is to inform online changes to the cost function or sampling during training: if it is observed during training that the classifier is very likely to confuse two classes despite there being relatively low uncertainty between these two classes, we could increase the output on the loss function for this error or over-sample from those two classes.
elements.sent-2411-024: In this way the collision matrix does not only indicates how well a classifier has learned a data distribution, but can also indicate how a classifier can be improved.
elements.sent-2411-025: To understand how our method relates to other UQ techniques, we next discuss how UQ techniques can be categorized and how the collision matrix fits into these categorizations (see Figure \ref{img:probabilitstic-vs-deterministic A ).\newline a) Uncertainty Type: Uncertainties involved with classification can be broadly divided into two types: epistemic and aleatoric \citep{AvsE1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Epistemic means relating to knowledge and describes the uncertainty resulting form a lack of knowledge such as insufficient training data or a lack of knowledge about the best model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-027: For example, Bayesian Networks \citep{Bayesian_Networks seek model uncertainty in the choice of model parameters which is epistemic.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Aleatory refers to chance and describes the uncertainty that cannot be reduced with more knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-029: For example, the Bayes Error Rate or BER \citep{Bayes_optimal is a measure of aleatoric uncertainty because it refers the unavoidable error even with access to the best possible classifier.}
elements.sent-2411-030: The collision matrix also measures the uncertainty inherent to a data distribution making it a property of the distribution itself independent of any classifier.\newline b) Description Metric: In addition to describing different types of uncertainty, different UQ techniques also use a variety of metrics to measure uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-031: Some of these metrics are very intuitive such as percent confidence or expected error rate, while other measures are more esoteric such as Shannon Entropy \citep{UQ_wentropy , the expected affect on a cost function \citep{label_wise_AandE or the cardinality of a prediction set \citep{conformal_intro .}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: We propose the new and intuitive measure: the expectation that multiple observations of the exact same input values will be found in different classes.
elements.sent-2411-033: When this expectation is large it means uncertainty is very high and no classifier could achieve high accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-034: On the other hand when this expectation is zero it means there is no inherent uncertainty at all---the setting is deterministic.\newline c) Description Level: A UQ measure may describe the uncertainty related to the data distribution itself (such as BER) or the uncertainty related a particular classifier (such as the familiar cross-validation accuracy or confusion matrix) or even for an individual prediction such as the techniques presented by \cite{bayesian_example .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Similar to the BER, the collision matrix describes the uncertainty arising from the distribution itself and not a specific classifier or prediction.
elements.sent-2411-036: Such measures can be used as an objective standard against which to judge the effectiveness of any classifier.\newline d) Description Detail: Some UQ techniques condense all uncertainty into a single values such as an the BER or entropy \citep{UQ_wentropy .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Other methods such as \citep{label_wise_AandE provide a more detailed description by providing uncertainty values for each class.}
elements.sent-2411-038: The collision matrix provides even more detail by describing uncertainty between each pair of classes.
elements.sent-2411-039: This level of detail is one of the key advantages of measuring uncertainty with the collision matrix and allows for a fine-grained evaluation of classifier performance.
elements.sent-2411-040: Because the collision matrix is a property of the data distribution, these uncertainties can can be interpreted as measuring the similarity between pairs of classes.
elements.sent-2411-041: Deriving further insights from the Collision Matrix.
elements.sent-2411-042: In a $K$-class setting the collision matrix will be a $K\times K$ matrix.
elements.sent-2411-043: The $(i,j)^{\text{th }$ entry of this matrix describes the difficulty of distinguishing elements of class $j$ from class $i$, which can be thought of as a measure of uncertainty or an indication of similarity between those classes.}
elements.sent-2411-044: For instance, ihe $i^{\text{th }$ column of the collision matrix indicates the difficulty of avoiding false negative classifications when the true class is $i$ indicative of the achievable recall on class $i$.}
elements.sent-2411-045: On the other hand, the $i^{\text{th }$ row describes how difficult it is to avoid false positive classifications into class $i$ related to the possible precision.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Finally, the diagonal elements of the collision matrix can be used to find a single value measure of the overall uncertainty in $\mathcal{D}$ related to the BER.
elements.sent-2411-047: More details in Section \ref{sec:BER and Figure \ref{fig:confusion .}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Summary of Contributions: In Section \ref{sec:defininition , we rigorously define a novel UQ measure, the collision matrix, as a stochastic matrix comprised of the class collisions rates between individual pairs of classes.}
elements.sent-2411-049: We also show how it can also be defined using the pdfs describing the data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-050: Additionally, we show how the collision matrix can be interpreted as the expected confusion matrix of a Probabilistic Bayes Classifier which is closely related to the Bayes Optimal Classifier, drawing a close link between the collision matrix and the BER.
elements.sent-2411-051: In Section \ref{sec:G_estimation , we examine the problem of estimating the collision matrix and develop an estimation method that takes advantage of the unique mathematical properties of the collision matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Specifically we show that show that under mild conditions the collision matrix (and all stochastic matrices) is uniquely defined by its Grammian Matrix $G$.
elements.sent-2411-053: We then show how $G$ can be approximated using a PAC learnable pair-wise comparison classifier.
elements.sent-2411-054: Finally, in Section \ref{sec:experiments we perform numerical evaluations of our method on synthetic and real world data sets.}
elements.sent-2411-055: We also estimate the collision matrix directly by using calibrated networks, MC dropout and Bayesian networks.
elements.sent-2411-056: Our results show that our Grammian based method provides better estimates of the collision matrix.
elements.sent-2411-057: Finally, we consider four real world datasets and show the new/additional insights that one can draw from the collision matrix.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Collision Matrix Estimation via Gramian and Pairwise Contrast}\label{alg:meta_algorithm} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Step A: Train a pairwise contrastive classifier $V$ using the difference training data defined in \eqref{eqn:diff_data}. \STATE Step B: Use $V$ to estimate the entries of the Gramian matrix $G$ using \eqref{eqn:gram_element_est}. \STATE Step C: Solve \eqref{eqn:ojective} via gradient descent to get the estimated collision matrix. \STATE Step D: (If necessary) Permute the rows of the collision matrix to achieve diagonal dominance. \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-151: The penalty term includes (a) the sum of the absolute difference between column sums of $\hat{S}$ and $1$ and (b) the sum of negative values of $\hat{S}$: \begin{equation}arg\min_{\hat{S}} || \hat{S}^T \hat{S} - \hat{G} ||_F \label{eqn:ojective} +\lambda\left (||\hat{S}^T1-1||_1 - \sum_{i=1}^K\sum_{j=1}^K \min\{\hat{S}_{i,j},0\}\right )\notag.\end{equation} Here $\lambda$ is an appropriately sized constant.

elements.sent-2411-154: In this section, we present experimental results to illustrate (a) our two-step method effectively estimates $S$, outperforming baselines, and (b) that the collision matrix provides useful insights into data not found by other UQ methods.
elements.sent-2411-155: For part (a) to show that we can estimate $S$ effectively we create a variety of synthetic Gaussian mixture data sets where we can calculate true posterior distributions and the ground truth $S$ matrix.
elements.sent-2411-156: We then estimate $S$ from sampled training data using baseline approaches and our Gramian based method.
elements.sent-2411-157: We compare the respective estimates from each method to the true $S$ and show an advantage for the Gramian method.
elements.sent-2411-158: For part (b), to show that $S$ provides useful insights, we estimate $S$ on a variety of real world data sets from different fields.
elements.sent-2411-159: We use $S$ to calculate the precision/recall values of the Probabilistic Bayes Classifier; this allows us to interpret the results in terms of what tasks may be accomplished with the data in these sets and what tasks are not possible with this data.

elements.sent-2411-181: Unlike single value uncertainty measures, such as the BER, the collision matrix informs us exactly what types of classification problems are inevitable in the data.
elements.sent-2411-182: For example, the uncertainty and class imbalances in the adult income data set will cause low precision for predicting high income such that a most of those predicted to have high income by the PBC will not have high income.
elements.sent-2411-183: These insights can have important practical implications: we found very low precision for diabetes (three quarters of those predicted to have diabetes will not have it), but we had much better recall with $67\%$ of individuals with diabetes being correctly classified.
elements.sent-2411-184: These values suggest: the information in this dataset is useful for identifying individuals who are at at high risk for diabetes and should undergo further testing (good recall), but the information in this data set is not sufficient to make trustworthy diagnoses (low precision).
elements.sent-2411-185: We emphasize that the collision matrix is a property of the data set, so training more sophisticated models on this dataset can not lead to high precision diagnoses, rather richer data (e.g., more informative features) could be necessary before a classifier can achieve high precision.
elements.sent-2411-186: On the other hand, if a classifier on this data set achieves recall for diabetes well below $67\%$, we know that it is possible to achieve better recall suggesting we adjust the classifier architecture or training procedure.
elements.sent-2411-187: This level of detail about what kind of classification performance is possible from a data set is a unique benefit of the fine-grained nature of $S$.
elements.sent-2411-188: We introduced the Collision Matrix as a new fine-grained approach for capturing aleatoric uncertainty in classification problems.
elements.sent-2411-189: Collision matrix provides a uniquely detailed description of uncertainty, measuring the difficulty in distinguishing between pairs of classes and providing unique insights for classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-190: We developed the fundamental properties of $S$; and leveraged those to devise a novel pairwise contrastive approach for estimating $S$.
elements.sent-2411-191: Furthermore, we validated the effectiveness of our estimation algorithm as well as showed the kind of insights that can be drawn from $S$ on several real-world datasets.

elements.sent-2411-193: Our work proposes a new statistic, the collision matrix, that describes the uncertainty inherent in a classification setting and provides an algorithm for estimating this collision matrix.
elements.sent-2411-194: This statistic provides a more fine-grained description or the uncertainty inherent to a dataset than previous methods and allows for prediction of the rates of particular classification types.
elements.sent-2411-195: Adoption of this statistic could help the practitioners of machine learning better understand the inherent limitations of prediction with specific datasets and help establish reasonable expectations.
elements.sent-2411-196: This metric would be of particular interest when costs involved with different types of classification errors vary significantly.
elements.sent-2411-197: The collision matrix could aid in understanding when more data or less specific predictions are required in order to achieve sufficiently trustworthy predictions.

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Collision Matrix Estimation via Pairwise Contrast}\label{alg:label_smoothing} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} $\mathcal{T} = \{\mathcal{T}_1,\mathcal{T}_2,\cdots,\mathcal{T}_K\}$ partitioned training data and learning rate $\eta$. \STATE { Step 1: Estimating Gramian Matrix $G$} \STATE Train binary contrast verifier $V$ on the difference training data $\mathcal{T}_{\text{difference}}$ (defined in \eqref{eqn:diff_data}). \FOR{$\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{T}_i$} \FOR{$\mathbf{\tilde{x}} \in \mathcal{T}_j$} \STATE Compute $G_{i,j} = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}_i|}\sum_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{T}_i} \frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}_j|}\sum_{\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \in \mathcal{T}_j} V(\mathbf{x},\tilde{\mathbf{x}})$ \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \STATE { Step 2: Estimating Collision Matrix $S$} \STATE { (a) Initialize:} $S = I$ (or any diagonally dominant matrix) \STATE { (b) Iterative approach:} \WHILE{$\|S^T S - \hat{G}\|_{F} > \gamma$} \STATE \begin{equation}S = S - \eta {\nabla}_S || \hat{S}^T \hat{S} - \hat{G} ||_F - \eta {\nabla}_S\left (\lambda||\hat{S}^T1-1||_1- \sum_{i=1}^K\sum_{j=1}^K \min\{\hat{S}_{i,j},0\}\right )\end{equation} \ENDWHILE \STATE return $S$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.subsubsection-2411-005: Gramian Based Algorithm Details and Complexity Analysis
elements.sent-2411-244: Algorithm \ref{alg:label_smoothing provides a detailed implementation of the meta-algorithm found in the paper (Algorithm \ref{alg:meta_algorithm ).}}
elements.sent-2411-245: Complexity: Algorithm \ref{alg:label_smoothing proposes estimating $S$ in two steps: (a) compute the Grammian $G$ and (b) recover $S$ from $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-246: We will compare the tasks involved in each of these steps (recall $K$ is the number of classes and $n$ is the number of points in the training data):.
elements.sent-2411-247: We first train the pairwise classifier $V$, then compute an empirical estimate for each of the $K^2$ elements of $G$ by calculating the average of a series of outputs from $V$.
elements.sent-2411-248: These empirical estimates require $n^2$ evaluations of $V$, one evaluation for each element of $\mathcal{T _{\text{difference }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-249: In practice, however, we found it was equally effective to pick a number $m$ of randomly selected elements to use in each average.
elements.sent-2411-250: This strategy requires only $mK^2$ evaluations of $V$.
elements.sent-2411-251: We run a $K^2$ dimensional gradient descent algorithm on optimization problem \eqref{eqn:ojective from the paper to find the our estimated collision matrix $\hat{S}$.}
elements.sent-2411-252: In practice the training of $V$ dominates all other tasks as it involves optimizing across all the parameters of $V$ (which includes far more than $K^2$ values) over the difference training data (which has $n^2$ elements).
elements.sent-2411-253: PAC-bounds from \cite{our_icml show that $V$ is learnable, however, do not indicate how complex the model $V$ should be nor how long it will take to train.}

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Baseline Collision Matrix Estimation Algorithm}\label{alg:baseline} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} $\mathcal{T} = \{\mathcal{T}_1,\mathcal{T}_2,\cdots,\mathcal{T}_K\}$ partitioned training data and learning rate $\eta$. \STATE Train classifier $M$ on the training data $\mathcal{T}$ to estimate posterior class probability distributions. \FOR{$\mathbf{k} \in \{1,2,...,k\}$} \STATE Compute $\mathbf{s}_{k} = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}_k|}\sum_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{T}_k} C(\mathbf{x})$ \ENDFOR \STATE return $S$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-263: We then test a variety of temperature scale parameters $T$.
elements.sent-2411-264: This involves dividing the logits of the model (output immediately before the soft-max layer) by $T$ and then finding the Expected Calibration Error (ECE) over the validation set.
elements.sent-2411-265: The value for $T^*$ that leads to the lowest ECE on the validation data is then selected and in all future evaluations of the classifier logits are divided by $T^*$ before the soft-max layer.
elements.sent-2411-266: This baseline technique has the least computational expense and only requires the training of a classifier using typical methods and then the calculation of the ECE for a variety of values $T$.

elements.sent-2411-295: Here it is very possible that two students with identical features could differ in whether they passed the BAR based off of how much they studied and even sheer luck in guessing answers.
elements.sent-2411-296: This means that class collisions are possible and aleatoric uncertainty is present.

elements.sent-2411-328: We propose a new and intuitive metric for aleatoric uncertainty quantification (UQ), the prevalence of class collisions defined as the same input being observed in different classes.
elements.sent-2411-329: We use the rate of class collisions to define the collision matrix, a novel and uniquely fine-grained measure of uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-330: For a classification problem involving $K$ classes, the $K\times K$ collision matrix $S$ measures the inherent difficulty in distinguishing between each pair of classes.
elements.sent-2411-331: We discuss several applications of the collision matrix, establish its fundamental mathematical properties, as well as show its relationship with existing UQ methods, including the Bayes error rate (BER).
elements.sent-2411-332: We also address the new problem of estimating the collision matrix using one-hot labeled data by proposing a series of innovative techniques to estimate $S$.
elements.sent-2411-333: First, we learn a pair-wise contrastive model which accepts two inputs and determines if they belong to the same class.
elements.sent-2411-334: We then show that this contrastive model (which is PAC learnable) can be used to estimate the Gramian matrix of $S$, defined as $G=S^TS$.
elements.sent-2411-335: Finally, we show that under reasonable assumptions, $G$ can be used to uniquely recover $S$, a new result on non-negative matrices which could be of independent interest.
elements.sent-2411-336: With a method to estimate $S$ established, we demonstrate how this estimate of $S$, in conjunction with the contrastive model, can be used to estimate the posterior class portability distribution of any point.
elements.sent-2411-337: Experimental results are also presented to validate our methods of estimating the collision matrix and class posterior distributions on several datasets.
elements.sent-2411-338: As Machine Learning (ML) classifiers are adopted in an ever growing number of fields, they are increasingly being applied in high risk and high uncertainty scenarios such as healthcare \cite{healthcare , finance \cite{finance , hiring\cite{hiring and more.}}}
elements.sent-2411-339: Many of these settings are inherently probabilistic (contain aleatoric uncertainty), meaning that the observed features do not uniquely determine the class of an input, but rather establish a posterior probability distribution over the possible classes.
elements.sent-2411-340: For example, in healthcare diagnostics, a doctor's observations and the preliminary tests from a patient's first visit may not determine the source of the patients symptoms with absolute certainty, but these data can determine which ailments are more or less likely including specifying a most likely illness.
elements.sent-2411-341: For high stakes decisions, it is important not only to identify the most likely class but also to understand how likely that classification is to be incorrect and what other classes are the most probable alternatives.
elements.sent-2411-342: This has lead to increased interest in the field of uncertainty quantification (UQ), which attempts to describe the uncertainty involved in a prediction task.
elements.sent-2411-343: Inside the field of UQ, many techniques have been developed which measure a variety of distinct phenomena using many different metrics (See Figure \ref{img:probabilitstic-vs-deterministic B).}
elements.sent-2411-344: We propose a new and intuitive metric for uncertainty measurement, class collisions and show how it can be used to describe general uncertainty in a classification task and the uncertainty associated with individual predictions.

elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Collision Matrix Estimation via Pairwise Contrast}\label{alg:collision_estimation} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} $\mathcal{T} = \{\mathcal{T}_1,\mathcal{T}_2,\cdots,\mathcal{T}_K\}$ partitioned training data and learning rate $\eta$. \STATE { {Part 1: Estimating Gramian Matrix $G$}} \STATE Step A: Train binary contrast verifier $V$ according to Section \ref{sec:pairwise_contrast}. \STATE Step B: Use $V$ to estimate the entries of the Gramian matrix $G$ using \eqref{eqn:gram_element_est}. \bindent \FOR{$\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{T}_i$} \FOR{$\mathbf{\tilde{x}} \in \mathcal{T}_j$} \STATE $G_{i,j} \gets \frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}_i|}\sum_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{T}_i} \frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}_j|}\sum_{\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \in \mathcal{T}_j} V(\mathbf{x},\tilde{\mathbf{x}})$ \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \eindent \STATE { {Part 2: Estimating Collision Matrix $S$}} \STATE Step C: Solve \eqref{eqn:objective} via gradient descent to get the estimated collision matrix. \bindent \STATE $S \gets I$ (or any diagonally dominant matrix) \WHILE{$\|S^T S - G\|_{F} > \gamma$} \STATE $\mathbf{a} \gets {\nabla}_S|| S^T S - G ||_F $ \STATE$\mathbf{b} \gets {\nabla}_S\left ( ||S^T\mathbf{1}-\mathbf{1}||_1- \sum_{i=1}^K\sum_{j=1}^K \min\{S_{i,j},0\} \right )$ \STATE $S \gets S - \eta (\mathbf{a}+\lambda \mathbf{b})$ \ENDWHILE \eindent \STATE return $S$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.section-2411-008: Estimating Posterior Distributions Using the Collision Matrix
elements.sent-2411-528: As noted in the introduction, the collision matrix describes the aleatoric uncertainty in the entire data distribution $\mathcal{D}$, however, in practical settings one may also wish to describe the uncertainty (and by extension the potential risks) associated with an individual input $\mathbf{x}$.
elements.sent-2411-529: The posterior class probability distribution (Definition \ref{def:true_probs ) fully encapsulating the aleatoric uncertainty in classifying $\mathbf{x}$.}
elements.sent-2411-530: Learning these posterior distributions, however, is a very difficult task due to the type of training data available in practical situations.
elements.sent-2411-531: In the real world training data has the form of $\mathcal{T}$ where it is labeled by the single class in which the point was observed (e.g. what disease was ultimately determined to cause the symptoms).
elements.sent-2411-532: These one-hot labels do not contain information on what other classes were probable candidates making it difficult to train models to estimate posterior distributions and to test the performance of such models once they've been trained.
elements.sent-2411-533: Before we describe the how collision matrix and class collisions may be used to estimate posteriors, we discuss existing posterior estimation methods.
elements.sent-2411-534: A simple strategy to estimate posteriors is to use the output of an ML classifier trained with traditional empirical risk minimization (ERM) methods.
elements.sent-2411-535: We refer to such classifiers as $M^{\boldsymbol{\theta :\mathcal{X}\rightarrow\mathcal{K}$, where $\mathcal{K}$ is the set of probability distributions over $K$ classes and $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ contains the parameters of the model.}}
elements.sent-2411-536: The problem with this method is that traditional ML training focuses achieving a high accuracy through minimizing the risk of the classifier defined as $\mathbb{E _{(\mathbf{x ,c)\sim\mathcal{D }\ell(M^{\boldsymbol{\theta (\mathbf{x ),c)$, where $\ell$ is a loss function designed as a differentiable analog for accuracy.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-537: Note that this risk depends only on the observed class $c$, but a good posterior estimate depends on all the class probabilities $\mathbf{y (\mathbf{x )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-538: This means that high accuracy does not imply that the model is effective as estimating posterior distributions, and classifiers trained for ERM have been shown to attempt to replicate one-hot outputs (be overconfident) \cite{overconfident_base,overconfident_driving,overconfident_bayes,overconfident_mixup,overconfident_variation .}
elements.sent-2411-539: A more suitable goal than maximizing accuracy is to minimize the expected deviation from the true posterior $\mathop{\mathbb{E _{(\mathbf{x ,c)\sim\mathcal{D }\ell(M^{\boldsymbol{\theta (\mathbf{x ),\mathbf{y (\mathbf{x ))$, where $\ell$ is now a loss function measuring the statistical divergence between distributions $M^{\boldsymbol{\theta (\mathbf{x )$ and $\mathbf{y (\mathbf{x )$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-540: Unfortunately, we cannot train for this directly because we do not have access to any posteriors $\mathbf{y (\mathbf{x )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-541: Previous techniques to address this concern and provide better posterior estimates can be broadly divided into two categories: post-hoc model augmentations \cite{temp_scale,platt,isotonic and Bayesian inspired ensemble methods \cite{Bayesian_Networks,mc_dropout .}}
elements.sent-2411-542: Post-hoc model augmentations focus on modifying the output of a normally trained model to improve its ability to estimate posteriors by improving the calibration of the model.
elements.sent-2411-543: A model is well calibrated if its confidence matches its actual performance, e.g. of all a model's predictions where it puts $80\%$ confidence on the top class, the model should be right $80\%$ of the time.
elements.sent-2411-544: There are a variety of method for improving calibration such as temperature scaling \cite{temp_scale , Platt Scaling \cite{platt and isotonic regression \cite{isotonic , however, it is important to note that being well calibrated is a necessary but not a sufficient condition for accurately estimating posteriors.}}}
elements.sent-2411-545: Clearly a model that predicts true posteriors will be well calibrated, but a model that does not accurately estimate posteriors could also be well calibrated.
elements.sent-2411-546: For example, a constant classifier outputting the prior distribution $M^{\boldsymbol{\theta (\mathbf{x ) = \boldsymbol{\pi}$ is well calibrated, but will not provide good posterior estimates as it does not use any of the information in $\mathbf{x}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-547: This implies methods for improving calibration may not converge to the true posterior.
elements.sent-2411-548: Bayesian inspired ensemble methods, on the other hand, seek to find a distribution over the parameters of the model $\boldsymbol{\theta}\sim\mathcal{H}$ with tools such as Bayesian neural networks \cite{Bayesian_Networks and MC Dropout \cite{mc_dropout .}}
elements.sent-2411-549: By sampling model parameters from $\mathcal{H}$ one can create an ensemble of classifiers.
elements.sent-2411-550: Feeding an input $\mathbf{x}$ into all the members of this ensemble allows one to create a distribution over which classes $\mathbf{x}$ is most likely to be classified into.
elements.sent-2411-551: Note that the posterior $\mathbf{y (\mathbf{x )$, describes the aleatoric uncertainty inherent to $\mathcal{D}$, and does not describe epistemic uncertainty which comes from a lack of knowledge about the best classifier to fit the distribution \cite{AvsE1,AvsE2,AvsE3 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-552: The distributions created by ensemble methods, however, describe both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty \cite{label_wise_AandE,AvsE4 , inhibiting them from accurately modeling true posterior distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-553: We propose an innovative method for estimating posterior distributions using the collision matrix laid out in Figure \ref{fig:outline .}
elements.sent-2411-554: Our method is based on comparing a point of interest $\mathbf{x}$ to many labeled data points from all classes.
elements.sent-2411-555: By comparing $\mathbf{x}$ to a variety of points across all classes, we are able to learn how well $\mathbf{x}$ fits into each class and avoid the tendency of conventional ML classifiers to focus only on the most likely class.
elements.sent-2411-556: The metric we use to compare $\mathbf{x}$ to other points is similarity score from Definition \ref{def:sim , leading to the following definition of expected similarity to each class.}
elements.sent-2411-557: To estimate this expected similarity score we may use the same pair-wise comparison model $V$ that we use to estimate $S$.
elements.sent-2411-558: We will also need a sets of comparison points $\{\tilde{\mathbf{x ^{(i;j)}\}_{j=1 ^m$ for $1\leq i\leq K$, where each point $\tilde{\mathbf{x ^{(i;j)}$ belongs to class $i$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-559: Before training $V$ we split $\mathcal{T}$ into training and validation sets and we choose the comparison points $\tilde{\mathbf{x ^{(i;j)}$ from the validation set to avoid bias introduced by running evaluations on data used in training.}}
elements.sent-2411-560: This leads to the equation for estimating the expected similarity score: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:approx_simvec} \hat{\mathbf{q}}_i(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^m V(\mathbf{x},\tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{(i;j)}).\end{equation}.
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width = 14cm]{Figures/posterior_estimation_outline.pdf} \caption{Outline of our pair-wise contrastive method for estimating class posterior probability distributions $\mathbf{y}(\mathbf{x})$ from one-hot training data: Starting from one-hot data $\mathcal{T}$, we create difference training data $\mathcal{T}_{\text{diff}}$ defined in \eqref{eqn:diff_data} consisting of pairs of inputs from either the same or different classes. We next use $\mathcal{T}_{\text{diff}}$ to train a pair-wise contrastive model $V$ from Section \ref{sec:pairwise_contrast}. The model $V$ and $\mathcal{T}$ are used to estimate the matrix $S$ (as described in Section \ref{sec:collision_algorithm}) and expected similarity scores $\mathbf{q}(\mathbf{x})$ according to \eqref{eqn:approx_simvec}. Finally we solve $\hat{S}^{-1} \hat{\mathbf{q}}(\mathbf{x})$ to produce estimated posterior $\hat{\mathbf{y}}(\mathbf{x})$.} \label{fig:outline}
elements.sent-2411-561: The vector $\mathbf{q (\mathbf{x )$ indicates how well $\mathbf{x}$ fits in with the points from each class, which intuitively relates to how likely $\mathbf{x}$ is to belong to each class.}}
elements.sent-2411-562: The expected similarity scores, however, are mathematically distinct from the true posterior distribution $\mathbf{y (\mathbf{x )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-563: Fortunately, we can connect the similarity scores $\mathbf{q (\mathbf{x )$ to the posterior $\mathbf{y (\mathbf{x )$ by decomposing the probability in \eqref{eqn:eprob_vec : \begin{equation}q_i(x) = \mathop{E}_{\tilde{x}\sim D_i}Sim(x,\tilde{x})\notag = \mathop{E}_{\tilde{x}\sim D_i}\sum_{j=1}^KP(\tilde{c}=c=j|\tilde{x},x) = \mathop{E}_{\tilde{x}\sim D_i}\sum_{j=1}^KP(c=j|x)P(\tilde{c}=j|\tilde{x})\label{eqn:indepencence} = \sum_{j=1}^Ky_j(x)\mathop{E}_{\tilde{x}\sim D_i}P(\tilde{c}=j|\tilde{x}) = \sum_{j=1}^Ky_j(x)S_{j,i}\label{eqn:systemofeq},\end{equation} where \eqref{eqn:indepencence follows from the fact that $(\mathbf{\tilde{\mathbf{x ,\tilde{c )$ is selected independently from $(\mathbf{x ,c)$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-564: Observe that \eqref{eqn:systemofeq provides us with a linear system of equations connecting the posterior $\mathbf{y (\mathbf{x )$ with the expected similarity scores $\mathbf{q (\mathbf{x )$ and the collision matrix $S$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-565: This is the key insight allowing us to use the collision matrix to estimate posterior distributions.
elements.sent-2411-566: We summarize this result in Theorem \ref{thm:decomp .. \newline\newline.}
elements.sent-2411-567: The posterior distribution $\mathbf{y (\mathbf{x )$, the expected similarity scores $\mathbf{q (\mathbf{x )$ and the collision matrix $S$ are related by the linear equation \begin{equation}\label{eqn:collision} \mathbf{q}(\mathbf{x}) = S^T \mathbf{y}(\mathbf{x}).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-568: When $S$ is non-singular (as guaranteed when $S$ is strictly diagonally dominant) we may write \begin{equation}\label{eqn:linear} \mathbf{y}(\mathbf{x}) = (S^T)^{-1} \mathbf{q}(\mathbf{x}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-569: Equation \eqref{eqn:linear provides us with simple and efficient way to transform an estimate of the similarity scores $\mathbf{q (\mathbf{x )$ to and estimate of the posterior distribution $\mathbf{y (\mathbf{x )$.}}}}}

elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{\textit{Estimating posterior $\mathbf{y}(\mathbf{x})$}}\label{alg:full} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Steps computed once for the distribution. \hrule \STATE $V \gets$ Train the pairwise contrastive model according to the method in Section \ref{sec:pos_and_sim}. \STATE $\hat{S}\gets$ Estimate the collision matrix using Agorithm \ref{alg:collision_estimation}. \hrule \STATE Steps computed for each input $\mathbf{x}$ \hrule \STATE $\hat{\mathbf{q}}(\mathbf{x}) \gets$ Create an estimate of the expected similarity scores ${\mathbf{q}}(\mathbf{x})$ using \eqref{eqn:approx_simvec}. \STATE $\hat{\mathbf{y}}(\mathbf{x}) \gets (\hat{S}^T)^{-1} \hat{\mathbf{q}}(\mathbf{x})$ Solve \eqref{eqn:linear} to estimate the posterior. \end{algorithmic}

elements.algorithm-2411-006: \caption{Collision Matrix Estimation via Pairwise Contrast}\label{alg:collision_estimation} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} $\mathcal{T} = \{\mathcal{T}_1,\mathcal{T}_2,\cdots,\mathcal{T}_K\}$ partitioned training data and learning rate $\eta$. \STATE { {Part 1: Estimating Gramian Matrix $G$}} \STATE Step A: Train binary contrast verifier $V$ according to Section \ref{sec:pairwise_contrast}. \STATE Step B: Use $V$ to estimate the entries of the Gramian matrix $G$ using \eqref{eqn:gram_element_est}. \bindent \FOR{$i$ between $1$ and $K$} \FOR{$j$ between $1$ and $K$} \STATE $G_{i,j} \gets \frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}_i|}\sum_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{T}_i} \frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}_j|}\sum_{\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \in \mathcal{T}_j} V(\mathbf{x},\tilde{\mathbf{x}})$ \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \eindent \STATE { {Part 2: Estimating Collision Matrix $S$}} \STATE Step C: Solve \eqref{eqn:objective} via gradient descent to get the estimated collision matrix. \bindent \STATE $S \gets I$ (or any diagonally dominant matrix) \WHILE{$\|S S^T - G\|_{F} > \gamma$} \STATE $\mathbf{a} \gets {\nabla}_S|| S S^T - G ||_F $ \STATE$\mathbf{b} \gets {\nabla}_S\left ( ||S\mathbf{1}-\mathbf{1}||_1- \sum_{i=1}^K\sum_{j=1}^K \min\{S_{i,j},0\} \right )$ \STATE $S \gets S - \eta (\mathbf{a}+\lambda \mathbf{b})$ \ENDWHILE \eindent \STATE return $S$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.algorithm-2411-008: \caption{Collision Matrix Estimation via Pairwise Contrast}\label{alg:collision_estimation} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} $\mathcal{T} = \{\mathcal{T}_1,\mathcal{T}_2,\cdots,\mathcal{T}_K\}$ partitioned training data and learning rate $\eta$. \STATE { {Part 1: Estimating Gramian Matrix $G$}} \STATE Step A: Train pair-wise contrastive model $V$ according to Section \ref{sec:pairwise_contrast}. \STATE Step B: Use $V$ to estimate the entries of the Gramian matrix $G$ using \eqref{eqn:gram_element_est}. \bindent \FOR{$i$ between $1$ and $K$} \FOR{$j$ between $1$ and $K$} \STATE $G_{i,j} \gets \frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}_i|}\sum_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{T}_i} \frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}_j|}\sum_{\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \in \mathcal{T}_j} V(\mathbf{x},\tilde{\mathbf{x}})$ \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \eindent \STATE { {Part 2: Estimating Collision Matrix $S$}} \STATE Step C: Solve \eqref{eqn:objective} via gradient descent to get the estimated collision matrix. \bindent \STATE $S \gets I$ (or any diagonally dominant matrix) \WHILE{$\|S S^T - G\|_{F} > \gamma$} \STATE $\mathbf{a} \gets {\nabla}_S|| S S^T - G ||_F $ \STATE$\mathbf{b} \gets {\nabla}_S\left ( ||S\mathbf{1}-\mathbf{1}||_1- \sum_{i=1}^K\sum_{j=1}^K \min\{S_{i,j},0\} \right )$ \STATE $S \gets S - \eta (\mathbf{a}+\lambda \mathbf{b})$ \ENDWHILE \eindent \STATE return $S$ \end{algorithmic}
